summary: The project has resolved 81 of 118 issues (69%), with most problem lists fully cleared, but progress is concentrated on three active proofs (q4, q5, q6) where 37 open issues remain. Solvability evaluations reveal a sharp split: q7 (58%) and q10 (57%) are highly tractable while q3, q5, and especially q8 (9%) appear structurally stuck. Resource usage is minimal today (2 runs, $0.19), suggesting headroom to intensify effort on the high-leverage problems.
problems: q4 carries the largest backlog with 22 open issues despite 23 resolved — verification churn is high relative to its 16% solvability score
q5 has 14 open vs 10 resolved and a low 14% solvability score, indicating an approach that is not converging
q8 has the lowest solvability score (9%) and may require a fundamentally different strategy or be effectively infeasible with current methods
Low solvability scores cluster on q3 (13%), q5 (14%), q4 (16%), q9 (16%) — nearly half the core problems are evaluated as hard
Today's activity (only 2 runs) is far below what is needed to clear 37 open issues, signalling under-utilized compute/effort
highlights: q6 is nearly complete with 15 resolved and only 1 open issue — closest to full closure among active problems
q7 (58%) and q10 (57%) have by far the highest solvability scores and represent the best near-term wins
All aim_problem_lists, erdos_problems, and first_proof_2 items are fully resolved, plus q1-q3 and q7-q9 in first_proof_1
Best proofs consistently reach 100-issue depth on claude_opus_5-4, showing a stable, capable solving pipeline
Cost efficiency is strong ($0.1948 across runs) leaving substantial budget to scale up targeted work

suggested_todos.title: Close out q6 by resolving its single remaining open issue
Prioritize a focused push on q7 and q10 given their high (57-58%) solvability scores
Diagnose q4's high open/resolved churn and triage its 22 open issues to find blocking root causes
Re-strategize or pause q8 (9% solvability) pending a new proof approach rather than burning effort
Increase daily run volume to match the 37-issue backlog given low current spend
Audit q5's stalled approach and consider switching proof strategy

mistakes: Repeatedly attempting low-solvability problems (q8, q3, q5) with the same strategy instead of pivoting when scores stay flat
Allowing verification churn on q4 where many resolutions reopen or fail re-verification, inflating the open count
Spreading limited daily runs too thin rather than concentrating on near-complete (q6) or high-yield (q7, q10) targets
Likely misjudging problem difficulty up front — open backlogs correlate poorly with solvability scores, suggesting effort allocation ignores the eval signal
Treating all problems as uniformly solvable on the same model/depth instead of tailoring depth and approach to per-problem tractability
